news_summary: Bosnian rock band Dubioza Kolektiv creatively challenges blind trust in artificial intelligence through their satirical music.
news_summary_fr: Le groupe de rock bosniaque Dubioza Kolektiv remet en question avec humour la confiance aveugle accordée à l'intelligence artificielle à travers sa musique satirique.
news_summary_es: La banda de rock bosnia Dubioza Kolektiv cuestiona de forma creativa la confianza ciega en la inteligencia artificial a través de su música satírica.

articles.title: Bosnian rock band Dubioza Kolektiv satirizes blind trust in artificial intelligence
articles.summary: Known for blending humor, political commentary, and catchy rhythms, this internationally popular band continues its tradition of addressing contemporary issues through satire.
articles.content:
The Bosnian band Dubioza Kolektiv has released a new song named “Yebiga,” (a common expletive similar to “Screw that,” translated as “What the hell” in the subtitles). The song offers a sharp and funny critique of society’s growing dependence on artificial intelligence (AI) and algorithmic decision-making, enabling a technocracy — (in the sense of rule by tech billionaire oligarchs) — instead of democracy.

Known for blending humor, political commentary and catchy rhythms, this internationally popular band continues its tradition of addressing contemporary issues through satire.

In the past, they have confronted media overload, democratic backsliding, migration to the US, online music piracy, Balkan stereotypes, the reality TV industry, the Flat Earth conspiracy theory, and more. This time, they are focusing on the embrace of technology and the erosion of independent thinking.

They published the video of the song in Bosnian, with English and Spanish subtitles:

The opening describes the rise of a digital brotherhood in which Elon Musk, Peter Thiel and Jeff Bezos symbolize the arrival of technocracy, setting the tone for a critique of systems increasingly governed by algorithms. In the chorus, the band ironically celebrates a world where AI replaces books and thinking altogether:

A.I. baš nas briga

ne treba nam više knjiga AI, svi smo isti

ne moramo više mislit’

AI, we don’t care

go and throw the books away (we don’t need books anymore) AI, we’re the same

we don’t need to use our brains (we don’t need to think anymore)

Through repetition and deliberately simplistic phrasing, the lyrics mimic the very mindset they criticize — one that prioritizes convenience over critical engagement.

The song’s central refrain, “AI is the boss now; there’s no going back…what the hell!” captures a sense of resignation toward technological dominance, while the use of humor and colloquial language underscores the absurdity of surrendering human agency to machines.

In another verse, the band contrasts physical labor with automated thinking, suggesting a world where humans perform routine tasks while machines make decisions:

Čovjek fizikaneri,

nek’ razmišlja mašina Ako mene pitaš

‘vako mi je draže

Radim tačno ‘nako

kako “kompijuter” kaže

Humans do the labor,

we will let machines think. If you ask me,

I prefer it much better this way —

I just do whatever

mighty computer says

This portrayal reflects broader concerns about algorithmic authority, echoing debates about automation, digital platforms, and the societal impact of artificial intelligence.

Reviving Yugoslav comedy gold

The music video, directed by Vedran Mujagić, reinforces these themes through a playful yet unsettling visual narrative, featuring exaggerated characters and scenes that highlight conformity and passive acceptance of technological control.

The video features the funny character Đipalo Junuz, played by Zenit Đozić, and the fictional invention HEPEK — a device composed of a log and electronic circuits.

Both were introduced in the legendary sketch comedy show “Top Lista Nadrealista” (“The Surrealists’ Top Chart”) or TLN for short, produced by TV Sarajevo between 1984 and 1991, and widely regarded as one of the most influential satirical shows in Yugoslav pop culture.

Part of the subcultural “New Primitivism” art movement, TLN was like a local version of “Monty Python's Flying Circus.” Many of its sketches, including HEPEK, became cultural memes decades before the internet.

During the tensions that led to Yugoslav Wars of the 1990s, HEPEK was satirically presented as a device that helped resolve conflicts — everything from kindergarten disagreements and disputes between neighbors over a cow’s grazing ground, to frustrations with bureaucracy and inter-ethnic tensions in parliament:

In the first such sketch from 1990, using pseudo-technical and exaggerated bureaucratic language, the character Đipalo Junuz explains that HEPEK is an invention developed in the supposed new technology center of Čeljigovići, which in reality was — and still is — a small rural village in the Bosnian mountains near Sarajevo. He claims that the villagers had invented “the splitting of atoms, artificial insemination and the Hrkljuš,” a fictional sport that also became a meme. Other sketches present the villagers as “economically and educationally” challenged: they use non-elite language, dress in old clothes, and live in modest homes.

In the words of Đipalo Junuz:

HEPEK, to ti je ko ono, kod situacija kada si čoek ono nešto na živčanom sistemu, ono nekako pobudali, kada se natakari na tebe, ti na njega djeluješ hepekom i situacija za čas se smiri.

Znači kada se on natakari na tebe, ti ga hepekiraš i situacija se smiri.

HEPEK, it's like that, in situations where you have done something to a person’s nervous system, you have somehow aroused it to become crazy, when he attacks you, you act on him with HEPEK and the situation calms down in an instant. So when he turns on you, you HEPEK him and the situation calms down.

In the new Dubioza Kolektiv video, different situations and dilemmas involving the use of AI are solved by applying the upgraded version of HEPEK; the song ends with the signature “HEPEK sound.”

This approach of reintroducing cult TV comedy elements resonated with the band’s audience: the YouTube version got over one and a half million views in a month, which is a lot for a song from the Western Balkans.

Dubioza Kolektiv has built a reputation across the region and beyond for tackling political and social issues — from nationalism and corruption to conspiracy theories and media manipulation. Their previous work has often intersected with disinformation themes, using satire to expose flawed logic and manipulative narratives.

With “Yebiga,” the band shifts its focus to a different but related domain: the risks of outsourcing critical thinking to opaque technological systems. In a region where media literacy and information integrity remain key challenges, such cultural interventions play an important role in encouraging audiences to reflect on their relationship with technology.

By framing complex issues through humor and accessible language, Dubioza Kolektiv once again demonstrates how popular culture can contribute to public awareness — this time by asking a simple but urgent question: “What happens when we stop thinking for ourselves?”
articles.reasoning: The article highlights an internationally popular band using satire and humor to raise public awareness about the societal risks of over-reliance on AI and loss of critical thinking. This cultural intervention has broad significance for society, encouraging reflection on technology's impact on democracy and human agency. The story is focused, detailed, and shows a positive real-world impact by promoting media literacy and critical engagement.
articles.personality_title: Bosnian band Dubioza Kolektiv uses satire to question AI's growing role in society
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Dubioza Kolektiv is a popular Bosnian rock band known for mixing humor and political messages in their songs. They often use satire to talk about important social issues, from corruption to conspiracy theories.

**What happened** – The band released a new song called “Yebiga,” which means “What the hell.” This song humorously criticizes how people are relying too much on artificial intelligence (AI) and algorithms to make decisions, instead of thinking for themselves. The music video includes references to famous tech billionaires and uses characters from a classic Bosnian comedy show to show how technology can take control over people’s lives.

**Impact** – This song stands out because it uses simple, funny lyrics and well-known cultural jokes to make a serious point about how AI might reduce human thinking and democracy. It has attracted over 1.5 million views on YouTube, showing that many people connect with its message. It encourages listeners to think carefully about technology’s influence on their freedom and choices.

**What's next step** – The band’s work may inspire more conversations about how technology affects society, especially in places where media education is still developing. By mixing entertainment with important ideas, Dubioza Kolektiv helps people become more aware and question the role of AI in everyday life.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Dubioza Kolektiv’s new song uses humor and culture to challenge blind trust in AI and urges people to keep thinking for themselves.
articles.personality_title_fr: Le groupe bosniaque Dubioza Kolektiv utilise la satire pour interroger le rôle croissant de l’IA
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Dubioza Kolektiv est un groupe de rock bosniaque célèbre pour mêler humour et messages politiques dans ses chansons. Ils utilisent souvent la satire pour aborder des problèmes sociaux importants, comme la corruption et les théories du complot.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le groupe a sorti une nouvelle chanson intitulée « Yebiga », qui signifie « C’est quoi ce bordel ». Cette chanson critique avec humour la dépendance excessive à l’intelligence artificielle (IA) et aux algorithmes pour prendre des décisions, au lieu de penser par soi-même. Le clip vidéo fait référence à des milliardaires de la tech célèbres et utilise des personnages d’une émission comique bosniaque classique pour montrer comment la technologie peut contrôler la vie des gens.

**Impact** – Cette chanson se distingue par ses paroles simples et drôles ainsi que ses blagues culturelles bien connues pour faire passer un message sérieux sur la réduction possible de la pensée humaine et de la démocratie à cause de l’IA. Elle a attiré plus de 1,5 million de vues sur YouTube, montrant que beaucoup de gens adhèrent à ce message. Elle encourage à réfléchir sur l’influence de la technologie sur la liberté et les choix.

**Prochaine étape** – Le travail du groupe peut susciter davantage de discussions sur l’impact de la technologie dans la société, surtout dans les régions où l’éducation aux médias est encore en développement. En mêlant divertissement et idées importantes, Dubioza Kolektiv aide les gens à mieux comprendre et questionner le rôle de l’IA dans la vie quotidienne.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La nouvelle chanson de Dubioza Kolektiv utilise l’humour et la culture pour défier la confiance aveugle en l’IA et invite chacun à continuer à penser par soi-même.
articles.personality_title_es: La banda bosnia Dubioza Kolektiv usa la sátira para cuestionar el creciente papel de la IA
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Dubioza Kolektiv es una banda de rock bosnia conocida por combinar humor y mensajes políticos en sus canciones. A menudo usan la sátira para hablar de temas sociales importantes, como la corrupción y las teorías conspirativas.

**Qué pasó** – La banda lanzó una nueva canción llamada “Yebiga”, que significa “¿Qué demonios?”. Esta canción critica con humor cómo las personas dependen demasiado de la inteligencia artificial (IA) y los algoritmos para tomar decisiones, en vez de pensar por sí mismas. El video musical incluye referencias a famosos multimillonarios tecnológicos y usa personajes de un programa clásico de comedia bosnio para mostrar cómo la tecnología puede controlar la vida de las personas.

**Impacto** – Esta canción destaca por sus letras simples y divertidas junto a bromas culturales conocidas para transmitir un mensaje serio sobre cómo la IA podría reducir el pensamiento humano y la democracia. Ha conseguido más de 1,5 millones de vistas en YouTube, lo que muestra que muchas personas conectan con su mensaje. Invita a reflexionar sobre la influencia de la tecnología en la libertad y las decisiones.

**Próximo paso** – El trabajo de la banda puede inspirar más conversaciones sobre cómo la tecnología afecta a la sociedad, especialmente en lugares donde la educación mediática aún está en desarrollo. Al combinar entretenimiento con ideas importantes, Dubioza Kolektiv ayuda a que la gente sea más consciente y cuestione el papel de la IA en la vida diaria.

**Conclusión en una frase** – La nueva canción de Dubioza Kolektiv usa el humor y la cultura para desafiar la confianza ciega en la IA y anima a las personas a seguir pensando por sí mismas.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of a rustic wooden log intertwined with glowing electronic circuits, symbolizing the fictional HEPEK device, surrounded by a group of diverse, faceless silhouettes passively connected to floating, softly glowing algorithmic symbols shaped like simplified computer chips, all set against a gentle Bosnian mountain village backdrop under a calm sky, capturing the tension between human tradition and surrender to AI-driven technocracy.
